title: Sterile vs Nonsterile Compounding: USP Standards and Caribbean Requirements
description: Pharmacy technician compounding involves preparing customized medications to meet specific patient needs. It is divided into sterile and nonsterile processes, both of which require strict adherence to United States Pharmacopeia (USP) quality and safety guidelines.Sterile CompoundingSterile compounding creates contamination-free medications, such as intravenous (IV) admixtures, injections, and eye drops. It relies on aseptic techniques to prevent microbial contamination.Primary Guidelines: Regulated by USP General Chapter <797>.Environment: Requires controlled cleanrooms, laminar airflow hoods, and ISO-classified environments.Advanced Roles: Technicians can earn the specialized CSPT Credential to demonstrate advanced knowledge in sterile environments.Nonsterile CompoundingNonsterile compounding involves creating customized oral liquids, topical creams, ointments, and capsules that do not require an environment free of microorganisms.Primary Guidelines: Governed by USP General Chapter <795>.Environment: Prepared in designated, clean, and well-lit areas with surfaces that are smooth, non-porous, and easy to clean.Advanced Roles: Technicians can advance their careers by completing training and earning the PTCB Nonsterile Compounding Certificate.Hazardous Drugs (HDs)Both sterile and nonsterile compounding can involve hazardous medications (like chemotherapy drugs).Primary Guidelines: Governed by USP General Chapter <800>, which outlines worker safety and environmental control requirements.Procedures: Involves the use of negative-pressure rooms, closed-system transfer devices (CSTDs), and specific Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) to protect the technician.Key RegulationsUSP Compounding Standards: Sets the public standards for preparing, packaging, and storing compounded medications to ensure consistent product quality.Beyond-Use Dates (BUDs): Technicians must know how to calculate and apply the appropriate BUD for each preparation, which dictates how long the medication remains stable and usable.
